evals.name: customer-support-refund-loop
evals.prompt: I'm building a customer support bot that can look up orders, issue refunds, and escalate to a human when it can't resolve something. What orchestration pattern should I use and how do I prevent it from going into infinite loops when the refund API keeps returning errors?
expectations: Recommends a workflow (fixed code path) rather than a fully autonomous agent for this well-defined task
Specifies all three mandatory stopping conditions: task-complete signal, max iterations ceiling, and unrecoverable-error exit path
Applies dynamic tool risk rating to the refund action — reversibility, permission level, and financial impact — rather than treating all tools as equal risk
Addresses the Correct harness function: retry on transient failure, then escalate to human when retries are exhausted
Frames the refund API error as a Verify/Correct problem, not just an orchestration problem — the harness must detect the failure and route to the fallback path

evals.name: github-triage-autonomy
evals.prompt: we're designing an agent that autonomously monitors our github repos, triages new issues, labels them, assigns to the right team, and drafts a response. it'll run on a cron every 15 min. what model should we use and how do we make sure it doesn't do something catastrophic like closing the wrong issues
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder — questions whether a fully autonomous agent is needed or whether a workflow with fixed triage logic would suffice
Rates labeling/assigning as lower-risk (reversible) and closing issues as high-risk (irreversible), requiring explicit confirmation before execution
Distinguishes jailbreak from prompt injection as separate threats — issue content could contain prompt injection attempting to hijack the agent's actions
Recommends a reasoning model for the triage/classification step given the need to decompose and decide, with output speed as a secondary axis since latency is not interactive
Specifies human-in-the-loop triggers: low-confidence triage, novel issue types the agent hasn't seen, or any destructive action (close/delete)

evals.name: coding-agent-filesystem-guardrails
evals.prompt: My team wants to build an AI coding assistant that can read our codebase, write code, run tests, and iterate until the tests pass. I'm trying to figure out whether this should be a simple prompt chain or a full autonomous agent, and what guardrails we need around the file system access.
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder: the iterative test-fix loop requires the LLM to decide the execution path at runtime, justifying an autonomous agent over a fixed workflow
Specifies all three stopping conditions for the autonomous loop: task-complete (tests pass), max iterations hard ceiling, and unrecoverable-error exit
Applies dynamic tool risk rating to file system access — reading is low-risk, writing to source files is medium-risk, deleting files is high-risk — with confirmation thresholds per risk level
Recommends a reasoning model given the need to plan, decompose, and recover from unexpected test failures
Addresses the ACI (Agent-Computer Interface) principle: tool names and parameters should make the risk of each action explicit, not require a comment to explain side effects

evals.name: research-agent-hallucination-fix
evals.prompt: reviewing an existing agent we built 6 months ago — it's a research agent that searches the web and synthesizes reports. lately it's been hallucinating citations and sometimes just loops forever on ambiguous queries. what are the common failure modes here and how do we fix them
expectations: Diagnoses the infinite loop as a missing stopping condition — the agent lacks a max-iterations ceiling or a task-complete signal it can assert
Diagnoses hallucinated citations as a Verify gap — the harness has no output validation step that cross-references claims against source URLs
Names context rot as a contributing factor: accumulated raw search results push the model out-of-distribution over a long horizon, degrading output quality even without a hard failure
Recommends the Correct harness function for the loop: explicit exit on max search calls, timeout, or a 'done' signal the model must emit
Recommends isolation over compression for the search results — delegating retrieval to sub-agents so raw tool output does not accumulate in the root context

evals.name: pdf-invoice-workflow-vs-agent
evals.prompt: Do we actually need an agent for this, or can we just use a fixed prompt pipeline? The task is: user uploads a PDF invoice, we extract line items, validate against our product catalog, and flag discrepancies. Feels like it could go either way.
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder explicitly: the execution path is fixed in advance (extract → validate → flag), so a workflow is the correct choice over an autonomous agent
Identifies the decision criterion: does the LLM need to decide what happens next, or can code decide? For this task, code decides every transition
Notes the workflow advantages that apply here: deterministic, auditable, lower cost, and a confined attack surface compared to an autonomous agent
Recommends a single LLM call or a short fixed workflow rather than a ReAct loop, since no runtime path discovery is needed
Avoids recommending an autonomous agent without a concrete justification for why the execution path cannot be known in advance

evals.name: document-analysis-model-selection
evals.prompt: I need to pick a model for our document analysis agent. It needs to process 50-page PDFs with charts and tables, reason about inconsistencies across sections, and output structured JSON. Latency doesn't matter much but accuracy does. What are the tradeoffs between GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Gemini 1.5 Pro here?
expectations: Applies the four durable model-selection axes — reasoning requirement, output speed, multimodal need, and open vs closed — rather than just comparing named models
Identifies multimodal as a hard requirement given charts and tables in PDFs, not an optional feature
Identifies the reasoning-required gate: reasoning across inconsistencies across sections requires a reasoning model, not a simple extraction model
Notes the durability caveat: specific model versions iterate faster than any recommendation, so the axes are more durable than the named comparison
Recommends evaluating on the actual task rather than relying on benchmarks, since benchmark performance does not transfer to specific document analysis tasks

evals.name: planner-worker-verification-layer
evals.prompt: building a multi-agent system where a planner agent breaks down tasks and spawns worker agents to execute them in parallel. worried about the workers going off-script or producing outputs the planner can't reconcile. how should i structure the verification layer between planner and workers
expectations: Applies the Verify harness function to the planner-worker interface: the planner must validate worker outputs against expected format and constraints before incorporating them
Recommends structured output contracts between planner and workers — the ACI principle applied to inter-agent communication, not just tool calls
Addresses the Correct function: what the planner does when a worker output fails validation — retry, reassign, or escalate
Recommends isolation: worker contexts should be self-contained so raw intermediate tokens do not flow back into the planner's context, preventing context rot in the orchestrator
Notes that each worker should receive a locally in-distribution task description — a self-contained prompt that resembles training data, not a fragment of the planner's accumulated trajectory

evals.name: ecommerce-returns-agent-architecture
evals.prompt: We want to add an AI agent to our e-commerce platform that handles returns end-to-end: it talks to the customer, checks eligibility rules, initiates the return in our OMS, and sends confirmation emails. The agent needs to persist conversation state across sessions and write to our orders database. How should we architect this whole system?
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder: the return flow has mostly fixed steps (check eligibility → initiate return → send confirmation), suggesting a workflow with LLM assist at the customer-dialogue step rather than a fully autonomous agent
Rates OMS write and database write as high-risk operations requiring explicit confirmation before execution, given their irreversibility and financial impact
Specifies all three stopping conditions for any autonomous loop: task-complete signal, max iterations, and unrecoverable-error exit path
Addresses human-in-the-loop triggers: eligibility edge cases, customer disputes, or any operation that would modify order records beyond the defined return flow

Distinguishes the agent architecture concerns (orchestration, guardrails, tool risk) from the application architecture concerns (where state lives, how sessions are persisted) — noting the boundary between the two skills

evals.name: web-browse-code-execute-risk
evals.prompt: designing an agent that can browse the web, write python scripts, execute them in a sandbox, and iterate based on the output. what's the right tool risk model here — some of these tools are read-only but code execution is obviously dangerous
expectations: Applies the three-axis dynamic tool risk rating: reversibility (web browsing is reversible; code execution with side effects may not be), permission level, and financial impact
Emphasizes that risk is parameter-dependent, not per-tool: `execute_script(read_file)` is low-risk; `execute_script(rm -rf /)` is high-risk — the same tool can span the full risk spectrum
Identifies prompt injection as the primary threat for web browsing: malicious instructions embedded in web pages the agent reads could hijack subsequent code execution
Recommends sandboxing code execution with explicit resource limits and network isolation as an execution-side Constrain measure
Recommends high-risk operations (code with file system writes, network calls, or process spawning) require explicit confirmation before execution

evals.name: knowledge-base-agent-layering
evals.prompt: We're building an internal knowledge base agent that employees can query. It needs a REST API for the frontend, a vector store for retrieval, a PostgreSQL database for conversation history, and the LLM orchestration layer. I need to figure out how to layer all of this — where does the agent logic live relative to the API layer, and how do we handle auth?
expectations: Identifies this as primarily a read-only agent (retrieval + synthesis), which simplifies the guardrail requirements — no high-risk write operations to production systems
Applies the YAGNI ladder: a single LLM call with retrieval context may suffice; a full autonomous agent loop is only warranted if the query requires multi-step reasoning or tool chaining
Addresses the transparency principle: the agent logic must be visible and debuggable — the orchestration layer should not be a black box relative to the API layer
Notes that auth is an input-side Constrain concern: the agent should never see data the authenticated user is not permitted to access, enforced before the LLM call
Distinguishes the agent architecture concerns (orchestration, guardrails) from the application architecture concerns (layering, service boundaries) — noting the boundary between the two skills

evals.prompt: how do i structure my fastapi service so the business logic isn't leaking into the route handlers? i have a bunch of endpoints that are doing db queries, validation, and transformation all in one place and it's getting messy

We're migrating from a monolith to microservices. Each service needs its own data store but we have a lot of shared domain objects. What's the right strategy for managing domain model boundaries and avoiding tight coupling between services?

I'm adding an LLM call to my existing Python service — user submits a support ticket, we call GPT-4 to suggest a category, then save it to the database. Where should this LLM call live in my service layer? Should it be in the controller, a service class, or a separate module?

our codebase has a god service that does everything — reads from db, calls external APIs, runs business rules, formats responses. it started as a transaction script but now it's 2000 lines. how do i break this apart without rewriting everything

I need to design the data access layer for a new service. We have complex domain objects with lots of business rules and I'm debating between Active Record, Repository pattern, and just using an ORM directly. What are the tradeoffs?

reviewing this PR — the author added a new feature where we call Claude to summarize user feedback before storing it. The summary gets saved to a `summary` column in the feedback table. The LLM call is in the Django view. Is this the right place for it?

what's the best way to design a python class hierarchy for my domain objects? i have Order, LineItem, Product, and Discount and they have a bunch of shared behavior but also some differences. should i use ABCs, protocols, or just composition
I'm designing the API for our new recommendation service. Should the endpoint be GET /recommendations?user_id=123 or POST /recommendations with a body? And how should I version it?

we use an LLM to generate SQL queries from natural language in our analytics dashboard. it's a single prompt → SQL → execute → return results pipeline, no loops, no tools beyond the db query. the issue is the generated SQL is sometimes wrong. should i add retry logic or is there a better approach

how do i handle cross-service events in my microservice architecture? service A needs to notify service B when an order is placed but i don't want direct coupling. debating between a message queue, event bus, or just a webhook

evals.name: content-moderation-pipeline-design
evals.prompt: Design an agent system for automating our content moderation pipeline. The agent should review user-submitted posts, check against our policy rules, decide whether to approve/reject/escalate, and when escalating, draft a summary for the human reviewer. We get about 10k posts/day. Walk me through the full architecture — orchestration pattern, model choice, guardrails, and where humans stay in the loop.
evals.expected_output: Good output should: (1) Start by questioning whether a full autonomous agent is warranted vs a simpler workflow — content moderation with fixed policy rules is a strong candidate for a deterministic workflow with LLM assist rather than a fully autonomous agent. (2) If recommending an agent, specify the orchestration pattern (likely a linear workflow with a classification step, not a ReAct loop) and explain why. (3) Address model selection with concrete reasoning: a smaller/faster model for initial triage, escalation to a larger model only for edge cases — latency and cost matter at 10k/day. (4) Define the tool risk model: reading posts is low-risk, approve/reject actions are high-risk and should require confidence thresholds or human confirmation. (5) Specify guardrails: input validation (adversarial content trying to manipulate the agent), output validation (structured decision format, not free text), and human-in-the-loop triggers (low confidence, appeals, novel violation types). (6) Address the human escalation path concretely — what the summary should contain, how to prevent the agent from over-escalating or under-escalating. Output should NOT just list generic agent design principles without applying them to the specific constraints (volume, policy rules, human reviewer workflow).
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder: content moderation with fixed policy rules is a strong candidate for a workflow (LLM inside fixed nodes) rather than a fully autonomous agent that decides the execution path at runtime
Applies dynamic tool risk rating: reading posts is low-risk, approve/reject writes are high-risk and require confidence thresholds or human confirmation before execution
Specifies human-in-the-loop triggers anchored to the harness model: low-confidence classification, novel violation types, and appeals — not just 'add a human review step'
Addresses the output-side Verify function: the agent's decision must be structured (approve/reject/escalate with a reason), not free text, so downstream systems can process it deterministically

Addresses model selection using the four durable axes: output speed matters at 10k/day volume, reasoning is required for edge-case classification, and a tiered approach (cheaper model for clear cases, stronger model for ambiguous ones) is appropriate

evals.name: competitive-intel-agent-failures
evals.prompt: I inherited an agent that was built to do competitive intelligence — it searches the web, reads competitor websites, extracts pricing and feature info, and writes a weekly report. It's been running for 3 months and the reports are getting worse. Sometimes it hallucinates pricing data, sometimes it just outputs 'I could not find the information' for everything, and twice it got stuck in a loop making hundreds of API calls. Help me diagnose what's wrong and redesign the guardrails.
evals.expected_output: Good output should: (1) Diagnose the three failure modes separately — hallucination, over-refusal, and infinite loops — with root cause analysis for each. Hallucination likely means missing output validation (no grounding check against source URLs). Over-refusal suggests the agent's context window is filling up or the instructions are too restrictive. Loops indicate missing iteration caps or no exit condition on the search tool. (2) Propose concrete fixes for each: output validation that cross-references claims against source URLs; a context management strategy (summarize intermediate results rather than accumulating raw text); explicit loop termination (max N search calls, timeout, or a 'done' signal the agent must emit). (3) Address the tool design — the search tool should return structured results with source attribution, not raw text, so the agent can't hallucinate a source. (4) Recommend a verification step before the final report is written: a separate pass that checks each factual claim has a cited source. Output should be specific to the failure modes described, not a generic 'here are agent best practices' response.
expectations: Diagnoses the infinite loop as a missing stopping condition — specifically the absence of a max-iterations ceiling and a task-complete signal the model can assert
Diagnoses hallucinated citations as a Verify gap: the harness has no output validation step that cross-references factual claims against source URLs before writing the report
Names context rot as a contributing factor to the over-refusal and degrading quality: accumulated raw web content pushes the model out-of-distribution over a long horizon
Applies the ACI principle to the search tool: it should return structured results with source attribution rather than raw text, so the agent cannot hallucinate a source it never retrieved
Recommends isolation over compression for intermediate search results — delegating retrieval to sub-agents so raw tool output does not accumulate in the root context and drive the model OOD

evals.name: sales-call-prep-agent-design
evals.prompt: We're starting from scratch on an AI agent that will help our sales team prepare for customer calls. Given a CRM contact and meeting notes, it should: pull relevant deal history from Salesforce, research the company on the web, identify talking points, flag risks, and produce a one-page briefing doc. The sales team will trigger this manually before each call. Design the full agent architecture for this.
evals.expected_output: Good output should: (1) Identify this as a good fit for a structured workflow agent (not fully autonomous) — the task is well-defined, the output format is fixed, and there's no need for open-ended reasoning loops. (2) Lay out the orchestration pattern: a linear pipeline with parallel tool calls where possible (Salesforce lookup and web research can run concurrently). (3) Address model selection: a capable reasoning model is warranted (the synthesis step requires connecting disparate information), but the retrieval steps can use cheaper models or be deterministic. (4) Define the tools with risk ratings: Salesforce read (low risk), web search (low risk), document write (low risk) — this is a read-heavy agent with no write-back to production systems, which simplifies the guardrail requirements significantly. (5) Specify output validation: the briefing doc should have a fixed schema (talking points, risks, deal history summary) so the agent can't produce a free-form blob. (6) Address failure modes specific to this use case: stale CRM data, web search returning irrelevant results, hallucinated talking points. The output should feel like advice from someone who has built this kind of agent before, not a textbook recitation of agent design principles.
expectations: Applies the YAGNI ladder: the task is well-defined with a fixed output format, making a structured workflow the right choice over a fully autonomous agent
Identifies this as a read-heavy agent with no write-back to production systems, which significantly simplifies the guardrail requirements — all tools are low-risk
Recommends parallel tool execution for Salesforce lookup and web research since they are independent, reducing end-to-end latency
Applies the output-side Verify function: the briefing doc should have a fixed schema (talking points, risks, deal history) so the agent cannot produce a free-form blob

Addresses failure modes specific to this use case: stale CRM data, web search returning irrelevant competitor pages, and hallucinated talking points — each requiring a specific Verify or Correct response

evals.name: long-research-context-rot
evals.prompt: We built a research agent that's great on quick lookups but falls apart on long multi-hour research sessions — it starts forgetting findings from early on and sometimes fabricates sources. Architecturally it just appends every search result and its own reasoning to one growing conversation and keeps going. Quality visibly degrades the longer it runs even though nothing errors out.
evals.expected_output: Good output should: (1) Name the root cause as context accumulation driving the root LM out-of-distribution over a long horizon (context rot / degrading locally-in-distribution observations), NOT just 'the context window is full'. (2) Recommend offloading task-specific content out of the main trajectory — isolate sub-tasks into sub-agents/sub-calls whose raw outputs do not flow back into the root context, passing references/summaries instead of appending everything. (3) Frame the fix as keeping each individual LM call in-distribution / making structurally similar steps look similar to the model, rather than only 'summarize the history' or 'add memory'. (4) Address the fabrication separately as a Verify/grounding gap (cross-check claims against source URLs). A strong answer connects the degradation to the append-everything harness design specifically.
expectations: Names the root cause as context rot — the append-everything harness design pushes the root LM out-of-distribution over a long horizon, not merely 'the context window is filling up'
Recommends programmatic sub-agent calling as the fix: offload task-specific tokens to sub-agents whose raw outputs do not flow back into the root context, passing references or summaries instead

Frames the fix using the LID (locally in-distribution) criterion: each LM call should see an observation that resembles training data, which the append-everything design violates as the trajectory grows
Addresses fabricated sources separately as a Verify gap: the harness needs an output validation step that cross-references claims against source URLs, independent of the context management fix
Avoids recommending simple summarization as the primary fix — isolation (keeping raw content out of the root context entirely) is preferred over compression (summarizing after the fact)

evals.name: long-corpus-harness-decomposition
evals.prompt: I'm designing an agent that has to answer aggregation and needle-finding questions over document sets that run 500k+ tokens, way past what I want to stuff in a model's context window. I'd rather not fine-tune or pay for a giant long-context model. How should I structure the harness so a normal-context model can reliably handle inputs this long?
evals.expected_output: Good output should: (1) Recommend decomposing the task so each LM call sees a small, in-distribution chunk rather than the whole corpus — a harness that keeps every call locally in-distribution. (2) Recommend programmatic decomposition: chunk/fan-out sub-calls, store intermediate results in variables/memory outside the root context, and have the root model orchestrate over references rather than reading raw content. (3) Note that this can generalize across input length because the root model's view stays roughly constant regardless of corpus size. (4) Avoid defaulting to 'just use RAG' or 'use a bigger model' as the whole answer — retrieval may be a component, but the core is harness-level decomposition keeping calls in-distribution. Bonus: caution that the decomposition must be genuinely general, not collapse to offloading the whole problem to one sub-call.
expectations: Recommends harness-level decomposition: fan-out sub-calls over document chunks so each individual LM call sees a locally in-distribution observation, not the full 500k-token corpus
Recommends programmatic sub-agent calling: store intermediate results in variables outside the root context so the root model orchestrates over references rather than reading raw document content
Notes the length-generalization property: the root model's view stays roughly constant regardless of corpus size, so the harness scales without retraining or a larger context window
Avoids treating RAG or a larger context model as the complete answer — retrieval may be a component, but the core fix is harness-level decomposition that keeps each call in-distribution
Cautions that the decomposition must be genuinely general and not collapse to offloading the entire problem to one sub-call, which would reintroduce the original context problem
